content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
1/ (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Michael C. Ghizzoni 
County Counsel 
County of Santa Barbara 
105 E. Anapamu Street, Suite 201 
Santa Barbara, CA 93101 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-013 
Dear Mr. Ghizzoni: 
February 10, 2017 
You are the County Counsel for the County of Santa Barbara and are requesting advice on 
behalf of County Supervisor Janet Wolf regarding the conflict ofinterest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of 
interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as 
common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
May County Supervisor Wolfs Second District staff persons who do not reside within the 
conceptual boundaries of a proposed Community Wildfire Protection Plan ("Plan") participate in 
Plan meetings and discuss this project (including any constituent concerns and complaints) with 
appropriate County departments so long as she is walled off from the meetings and discussions? 
CONCLUSION 
Supervisor Wolfs staff is not prohibited from participating in Plan meetings and discussing 
this project (including any constituent concerns and complaints) with appropriate County 
departments so long as the supervisor is walled off from the meetings and discussions. 
FACTS 
Janet Wolf was elected to represent Santa Barbara County's Second Supervisorial District. 
As required by its Eastern Goleta Valley Community Plan, Santa Barbara County is supporting 
development of a "Community Wildfire Protection Plan" ("Plan") for its San Marcos Pass/Eastern 
Goleta Valley Mountainous Areas. Although subject to change, the conceptual boundaries of the 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FileNo. A-17-013 
Page No. 2 
proposed Plan presently include Supervisor Wolfs residence and are estimated by the County 
Surveyor to include more than 40% of the land in the Second Supervisorial District. 
On March 16, 2016, a resident of the Second Supervisorial District informed Supervisor 
Wolf that some residents wanted to go forward with a Plan consistent with the Eastern Goleta 
Valley Community Plan and sought to discuss what County resources might be available. On 
April 8, 2016, that same resident informed Supervisor Wolf that a "steering committee" of residents 
had expanded the proposed Plan's area to include the "foothills" area; this would include 
Supervisor Wolfs home. On July 18, 2016, the County Fire Department issued a Request for 
Proposals ("RFP") for a consultant to prepare this Plan. The RFP stated, the boundaries of the 
project area are conceptual and should be refined during the Plan development process. On 
October 28, 2016, County Fire entered into a contract for Geo Elements LLC to assist with this 
work. The contract's Scope of Work included a requirement that the consultant work with County 
Fire, the U.S. Forest Service and interested parties to determine if the Plan boundaries needed 
refinement. 
Second District staff persons have represented the Second District in Plan meetings with 
County Staff and community members. Some of the duties of the Second District's staff include 
receiving and coordinating responses to constituent phone calls, e-mails and letters. On 
December 20, 2016, an attorney representing some residents within the proposed Plan asserted that, 
because Supervisor Wolfs home is within the proposed Plan area, neither Supervisor Wolf nor her 
staff should participate in the Plan process. That attorney had previously sent Supervisor Wolf the 
e-mail of April 8, 2016 -- discussed above - informing Supervisor Wolf that he and some residents 
of one part of the Second District had decided to include Supervisor Wolfs neighborhood in the 
proposed Plan boundary. 
A Community Wildfire Protection Plan is: 
• Developed within the context of collaborative agreements; 
• Identifies and prioritizes areas for hazardous fuel reduction treatments; 
• Recommends measures to reduce structural ignitability; and 
• "[I]s agreed to by the applicable local government, local fire department and State agency 
responsible for forest management, in consultation with interested parties and the Federal 
land management agencies managing land in the vicinity of the at-risk community." 
(16 u.s.c. § 6511 (3).) 
In providing Federal financial assistance for hazardous fuel reduction projects on non­
Federal land, the Healthy Forests Restoration Act of2003 states that the Secretary should give 
priority to communities that have adopted a Community Wildfire Protection Plan. (16 U.S.C. § 
6513(d)(2).) 
When the County approved the separate Mission Canyon Community Wildfire Protection 
Plan in 2011 within another Supervisorial District, the Notice of Exemption under the California 
Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) stated that approval of that plan did not: 


File No. A-17-013 
Page No. 3 
• Commit any signing party or anyone to carrying out, funding, or issuing a permit for any 
specific hazardous fuel reduction; 
• Require implementation of any of the recommendations within that plan; or 
• Remove any permit requirement, environmental review requirement, or other legal 
requirement applicable to hazardous fuel reduction on either private lands or public lands. 
Supervisor Wolf believes she does not have a conflict of interest concerning the proposed 
Plan because she does not believe the Plan will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect upon her home. This is in part because these types of plans are primarily informational 
documents that may or may not result in an area receiving more Federal funds for hazardous fuel 
reduction projects. Supervisor Wolf does not want the potential inclusion or exclusion of her home 
from the proposed plan's boundaries to distract from what should be a collaborative and inclusive 
process. She therefore has recused herself from future participation or decisions for this proposed 
plan. However, Supervisor Wolf believes it is important that all residents of the Second District 
continue to have access to her office and be able to be part of an inclusive process for the proposed 
plan. 
Supervisor Wolf would like her staff to be able to assist residents with this process. 
Supervisor Wolf would like to know if Second District staff persons who do not reside within the 
conceptual boundaries of the proposed plan and do not have any financial interests that would be 
affected can participate in plan meetings and discuss this project (including any constituent 
concerns and complaints) with appropriate County departments so long as she is walled off from the 
meetings and discussions. 
ANALYSIS 
As we have previously advised with respect to a different decision, even if Supervisor Wolf 
has a conflict of interest in participating in a decision, her staff would not be prohibited so long as 
they do not have any financial interests involved in the decisions. ( Wolf Advice Letter, No. A-16-
028.) You have indicated that Supervisor Wolfs staff does not have any financial interests that 
could be affected. Therefore, as long as Supervisor Wolfremains walled off, her staff may assist her 
constituents. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
-•--■al Counsel 
SK.B:jgl

May County Supervisor Wolfs Second District staff persons who do not reside within the 
conceptual boundaries of a proposed Community Wildfire Protection Plan ("Plan") participate in

appropriate County departments so long as she is walled off from the meetings and discussions?
sections.conclusion:
Supervisor Wolfs staff is not prohibited from participating in Plan meetings and discussing 
this project (including any constituent concerns and complaints) with appropriate County 
departments so long as the supervisor is walled off from the meetings and discussions.
